problem_statement.Title: --notes option ignores punctuation-only note tags (W0511)
problem_statement.Description: Punctuation-only note tags configured via --notes (for example '???') are not detected and do not produce fixme warnings (W0511). The issue occurs when comments contain tags made entirely of non-word characters.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. pylint/checkers/misc.py::EncodingChecker.open - The code constructs the fixme regex with a trailing word-boundary anchor "\\b" (examples: regex_string = rf"#\\s*({notes}|{self.config.notes_rgx})\\b" or rf"#\\s*({notes})\\b"). This encodes the incorrect assumption that note tags end on a word boundary; "\\b" fails to match tokens composed only of non-word characters. Example: --notes="YES,???" -> re.escape("???") => "\\?\\?\\?", pattern becomes "#\\s*(YES|\\?\\?\\?)\\b"; in a comment like "# ???: no" the character after "???" is ":" (a non-word char), so "\\b" does not match and the tag is missed.

2. pylint/checkers/misc.py::EncodingChecker.process_tokens - This method uses the compiled pattern (self._fixme_pattern.search("#" + comment_text.lower())) to decide whether to emit W0511. Because the pattern was built with "\\b", punctuation-only note tags that fail to match will never cause add_message("fixme") to be called, producing the observed missing warning.

3. pylint/utils/pragma_parser.py::MESSAGE_STRING - parse_pragma only recognizes pragma message tokens matching the regex "[0-9A-Za-z\-\_]{2,}". This encodes the assumption that pragma tokens are alphanumeric/hyphen/underscore and at least 2 characters, so punctuation-only tokens (e.g. "???") are not parsed from inline "# pylint:" pragmas. As a consequence, inline disables like "# pylint: disable=???" will not suppress punctuation-only notes even if detection is fixed.

4. Correct relationship for the regex anchor (diagnostic): the trailing "\\b" is the wrong hardcoded anchor for this use. The correct anchor for allowing punctuation-only tags while still preventing inside-word matches is a negative lookahead asserting the next character is not a word char: "(?!\\w)". The corresponding regex forms are: rf"#\\s*({notes}|{self.config.notes_rgx})(?!\\w)" or rf"#\\s*({notes})(?!\\w)".
StepsToReproduce: Create a file test.py with these two lines:
# YES: yes
Run: pylint test.py --notes="YES,???"
Observe which W0511 fixme warnings are emitted
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior:
Pylint emits fixme warnings (W0511) for both comment lines. Example output should include:
- test.py:1:1: W0511: YES: yes (fixme)
- test.py:2:1: W0511: ???: no (fixme)
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Only the first line with the word-like tag is reported. Example output contains:
- test.py:1:1: W0511: YES: yes (fixme)
The punctuation-only tag ("???") is not reported as a fixme warning.

hints_text: Did a little investigation, this is we're actually converting this option in a regular expression pattern (thereby making it awfully similar to the `notes-rgx` option). Since `?` is a special character in regex this doesn't get picked up. Using `\?\?\?` in either `notes` or `notes-rgx` should work.

problem_statement.Title: Traceback printed for unrecognized CLI option (pylint -Q)
problem_statement.Description: When invoking the pylint CLI with an unrecognized option (example: `pylint -Q`), pylint prints the user-facing error E0015 but then a Python traceback is emitted (pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError), which is not user-friendly.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) pylint/config/config_initialization.py::_config_initialization — emits the human-readable message via linter.add_message('unrecognized-option', ...) and then raises _UnrecognizedOptionError(options=unrecognized_options). (Incorrect assumption: emitting the message is sufficient and caller will handle the raised exception.)

2) pylint/__init__.py:run_pylint — the top-level CLI wrapper only catches KeyboardInterrupt and allows non-KeyboardInterrupt exceptions (including _UnrecognizedOptionError) to propagate to the interpreter, causing a traceback to be printed. (Incorrect assumption: non-KeyboardInterrupt exceptions should be allowed to bubble to top-level for CLI usage.)

3) pylint/lint/run.py:Run.__init__ — intentionally does not catch _UnrecognizedOptionError so programmatic callers and tests can observe the exception (tests use with pytest.raises(_UnrecognizedOptionError)). (This is an existing and intentional behavior and encodes the assumption that API callers should receive the exception.)

4) Exit-code convention — pylint/config/config_initialization.py already uses sys.exit(32) for config/usage errors. The investigation identifies exit code 32 as the existing, correct convention to represent configuration/usage errors (current behaviour elsewhere), so any CLI-level termination for unrecognized options should be consistent with that value.

5) Audit gap — other top-level entrypoints (run_epylint, run_pyreverse, run_symilar in pylint.__init__) were not inspected and may encode the same assumption (i.e., they may also fail to catch _UnrecognizedOptionError), so the issue could exist in multiple console wrappers.
StepsToReproduce: Open a shell with pylint available.
Run: pylint -Q
Observe the output: the E0015 unrecognized-option message is printed, followed by a Python traceback ending in pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The CLI prints a concise, user-facing error such as "E0015: Unrecognized option found: Q (unrecognized-option)" (optionally with a short usage hint) and exits without printing a Python traceback. The process should exit with a non-zero exit code consistent with existing config/usage error conventions (exit code 32).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: pylint prints the E0015 message for the unrecognized option, then a full Python traceback is shown (ending in pylint.config.exceptions._UnrecognizedOptionError) originating from the top-level entrypoint, which is not user-friendly.

problem_statement.Title: `--recursive=y` ignores `ignore-paths`
problem_statement.Description: When running pylint with --recursive=y, directories matching ignore-paths from configuration (e.g. "^src/gen/.*$") are still expanded and linted instead of being skipped.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) _expand_files passes an uninitialized attribute to expand_modules: pylint/lint/pylinter.py:PyLinter._expand_files calls expand_modules(..., self._ignore_paths) while self._ignore_paths has not been set yet.  
2) open() initializes the attribute too late: pylint/lint/pylinter.py:PyLinter.open assigns self._ignore_paths = self.linter.config.ignore_paths, encoding the incorrect timing assumption that callers will only need ignore_paths after open() is called.  
3) The expansion runs before open(): control flow (PyLinter.check -> _iterate_file_descrs -> _expand_files) shows module/package expansion occurs earlier than PyLinter.open(), so _expand_files receives an empty/incorrect ignore-path list.  
4) expand_modules expects the compiled ignore-path patterns as its last argument: pylint/lint/expand_modules.py:expand_modules uses the provided ignore_list_paths_re to filter filesystem elements; if that argument is empty or wrong, matching directories (e.g. src/gen) will not be skipped.  
5) The authoritative, correctly-typed value already exists in the parsed config as self.config.ignore_paths (produced by the regexp_paths_csv transformer), so the correct relationship is: pass self.config.ignore_paths to expand_modules at expansion time (instead of self._ignore_paths).
StepsToReproduce:
Create a pyproject.toml (or other config) with ignore-paths including a pattern that matches a generated subtree (example):
[tool.pylint.MASTER]
ignore-paths = ["^src/gen/.*$"]
Ensure your project contains src/ and a src/gen/ subtree with Python files (e.g. src/gen/about.py).
Run: pylint --recursive=y src/
Observe that files under src/gen/ are linted and reported instead of being skipped.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Files and directories that match ignore-paths from the configuration (for example any path matching "^src/gen/.*$") are excluded from recursive expansion and are not linted or reported.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Files under src/gen/ are expanded and linted when running pylint --recursive=y src/. Pylint reports many messages from src/gen/* (e.g. src/gen/about.py, src/gen/design.py, src/gen/settings.py, src/gen/resources_rc.py) despite ignore-paths containing a pattern that should exclude them.

Hmm I can reproduce your error, and now I understand the root cause. The root cause is following. The decision of skipping the path is here:

https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/3c5eca2ded3dd2b59ebaf23eb289453b5d2930f0/pylint/lint/pylinter.py#L600-L607

* When you execute pylint with `src/` argument following variables are present:
```python
(Pdb) p root
'src/gen'
(Pdb) p self.config.ignore_paths
[re.compile('^src\\\\gen\\\\.*$|^src/gen/.*$')]
```

* When you uexecute pylint with `.` argument following variables are present:
```python
(Pdb) p root
'./src/gen'
(Pdb) p self.config.ignore_paths
[re.compile('^src\\\\gen\\\\.*$|^src/gen/.*$')]
```

In the second case, the source is prefixed with `./` which causes that path is not matched. The simple fix should be to use  `os.path.normpath()` https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.normpath

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py b/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
--- a/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
+++ b/pylint/lint/expand_modules.py
@@ -82,8 +82,10 @@ def expand_modules(
             continue
         module_path = get_python_path(something)
         additional_search_path = [".", module_path] + path
-        if os.path.exists(something):
-            # this is a file or a directory
+        if os.path.isfile(something) or os.path.exists(
+            os.path.join(something, "__init__.py")
+        ):
+            # this is a file or a directory with an explicit __init__.py
             try:
                 modname = ".".join(
                     modutils.modpath_from_file(something, path=additional_search_path)
@@ -103,9 +105,7 @@ def expand_modules(
                 )
                 if filepath is None:
                     continue
-            except (ImportError, SyntaxError) as ex:
-                # The SyntaxError is a Python bug and should be
-                # removed once we move away from imp.find_module: https://bugs.python.org/issue10588
+            except ImportError as ex:
                 errors.append({"key": "fatal", "mod": modname, "ex": ex})
                 continue
         filepath = os.path.normpath(filepath)

problem_statement.Title: pylint fails with parse-error when directory contains a same-named module file (tries to parse non-existent __init__.py)
problem_statement.Description: Running pylint on a directory (e.g., `pylint a`) that contains a file named with the same module name (`a/a.py`) but no package initializer causes pylint to attempt to parse a non-existent file `a/__init__.py`, producing an F0010 parse-error and failing the lint run.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) expand_modules.expand_modules (pylint/lint/expand_modules.py:expand_modules) unconditionally computes filepath = os.path.join(directory, "__init__.py") for a directory argument and later appends a ModuleDescription whose "path" is that filepath even when that file does not exist. This causes PyLinter to receive and try to parse a bogus path (e.g., "a/__init__.py").

2) The code path that appends the ModuleDescription uses the computed filepath directly (result.append({..."path": filepath, ...})) without checking os.path.isfile(filepath). The exact relationship in the current code is: if os.path.isdir(something): filepath = os.path.join(something, "__init__.py") else: filepath = something; then result.append(... path=filepath ...). That relationship encodes the incorrect assumption that a directory argument always corresponds to an actual package file at __init__.py.

3) get_python_path (pylint/lint/expand_modules.py:get_python_path) encodes the same incorrect package-root assumption: its implementation walks parent directories while os.path.exists(os.path.join(dirname, "__init__.py")) and its docstring explicitly documents the (bad) assumption that __init__.py is always present. This makes package-root discovery dependent on the hardcoded string "__init__.py" and its existence.

4) PyLinter consumers assume paths produced by expand_modules are real files and do not validate existence before parsing (pylint/lint/pylinter.py: PyLinter._iterate_file_descrs -> get_ast -> MANAGER.ast_from_file). Because expand_modules can yield a non-existent "path" value, PyLinter forwards that path to astroid and triggers the observed parse-error.

5) expand_modules assumes modutils.modpath_from_file/_modpath_from_file will always return a usable modpath for subfiles and does not guard per-subfile conversions. The code computes modpath = _modpath_from_file(subfilepath, is_namespace, path=additional_search_path) and then submodname = ".".join(modpath) without try/except fallback; if modutils fails (ImportError or similar), no safe per-subfile fallback naming is provided, which can break expansion for submodules in namespace/edge layouts.

6) The hardcoded string "__init__.py" is the key incorrect constant: the code treats "__init__.py" as the package file without verifying existence. The correct relationship (as evidenced by the investigation) is: only treat os.path.join(dir, "__init__.py") as the package filepath when os.path.isfile(os.path.join(dir, "__init__.py")) is true; otherwise, the directory should be treated as a namespace/plain directory and its actual .py files should be enumerated as module entries, with robust fallback naming for subfiles when modpath computation fails.
StepsToReproduce: mkdir a && touch a/a.py a/b.py
Run: pylint a

Observe parse-error referencing a/__init__.py (example output: "a/__init__.py:1:0: F0010: error while code parsing: Unable to load file a/__init__.py: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'a/__init__.py' (parse-error)").
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Running `pylint a` on a directory that contains files (including a file named a.py) but no __init__.py should not attempt to parse a non-existent a/__init__.py and should successfully analyze the actual .py files in the directory (no F0010 parse-error caused by missing a/__init__.py).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: pylint attempts to parse a non-existent a/__init__.py and emits an F0010 parse-error (No such file or directory), causing the lint run to fail. Example output: "a/__init__.py:1:0: F0010: error while code parsing: Unable to load file a/__init__.py: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'a/__init__.py' (parse-error)".

diff --git a/pylint/config/argument.py b/pylint/config/argument.py
--- a/pylint/config/argument.py
+++ b/pylint/config/argument.py
@@ -99,11 +99,20 @@ def _py_version_transformer(value: str) -> tuple[int, ...]:
     return version
 
 
+def _regex_transformer(value: str) -> Pattern[str]:
+    """Return `re.compile(value)`."""
+    try:
+        return re.compile(value)
+    except re.error as e:
+        msg = f"Error in provided regular expression: {value} beginning at index {e.pos}: {e.msg}"
+        raise argparse.ArgumentTypeError(msg)
+
+
 def _regexp_csv_transfomer(value: str) -> Sequence[Pattern[str]]:
     """Transforms a comma separated list of regular expressions."""
     patterns: list[Pattern[str]] = []
     for pattern in _csv_transformer(value):
-        patterns.append(re.compile(pattern))
+        patterns.append(_regex_transformer(pattern))
     return patterns
 
 
@@ -130,7 +139,7 @@ def _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer(value: str) -> Sequence[Pattern[str]]:
     "non_empty_string": _non_empty_string_transformer,
     "path": _path_transformer,
     "py_version": _py_version_transformer,
-    "regexp": re.compile,
+    "regexp": _regex_transformer,
     "regexp_csv": _regexp_csv_transfomer,
     "regexp_paths_csv": _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer,
     "string": pylint_utils._unquote,

+            exit=False,
+        )
+    output = capsys.readouterr()
+    assert (
+        r"Error in provided regular expression: [\p{Han}a-z_][\p{Han}a-z0-9_]{2,30}$ beginning at index 1: bad escape \p"
+        in output.err
+    )
+
+
+def test_csv_regex_error(capsys: CaptureFixture) -> None:
+    """Check that we correctly error when an option is passed and one
+    of its comma-separated regular expressions values is an invalid regular expression.
+    """
+    with pytest.raises(SystemExit):
+        Run(
+            [str(EMPTY_MODULE), r"--bad-names-rgx=(foo{1,3})"],
+            exit=False,
+        )
+    output = capsys.readouterr()
+    assert (
+        r"Error in provided regular expression: (foo{1 beginning at index 0: missing ), unterminated subpattern"
+        in output.err
+    )
+
+
 def test_short_verbose(capsys: CaptureFixture) -> None:
     """Check that we correctly handle the -v flag."""
     Run([str(EMPTY_MODULE), "-v"], exit=False)

problem_statement.Title: pylint crashes when .pylintrc contains regex with Unicode property escapes (e.g. \p{Han})
problem_statement.Description: When a configuration option in .pylintrc contains a regular expression using Unicode property escapes (example: \p{Han}), running pylint raises an unhandled re.error during configuration parsing and fails to start.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Option/validator compilation assumes builtin re can compile all user regexes: pylint/config/option.py (VALIDATORS['regexp'], _regexp_validator, _regexp_csv_validator) call re.compile(value) unconditionally. Evidence: validators return re.compile(pattern) and the stack ends with re.error: bad escape \p at position 1. Correct relationship: user-supplied pattern must be compiled using a safe compiler that first tries re.compile(pattern) and, on re.error, may fall back to regex.compile(pattern) (if the third-party 'regex' module is available).

2) Argument transformers likewise assume re only: pylint/config/argument.py (_TYPE_TRANSFORMERS['regexp'], _regexp_csv_transfomer, _regexp_paths_csv_transfomer) use re.compile directly and propagate re.error to argparse. Location encodes the incorrect assumption that re.compile(...) is the sole valid compilation method for user regexes.

3) Consumers recompile or assume re.Pattern specifically: pylint/checkers/base/name_checker/checker.py (NameChecker.open) calls re.compile(rgxp) on config values, assuming values are strings or re.Pattern; this breaks when validators return compiled patterns from another engine. The incorrect assumption is that re.compile can accept or should be called on already-compiled pattern objects.

4) Serialization/serialization-checks assume only re.Pattern: pylint/config/utils.py (_parse_rich_type_value), pylint/config/arguments_manager.py (TOML generation), and doc/exts/pylint_options.py use isinstance(value, re.Pattern) (and similar checks for list elements) to detect compiled regexes. This encodes the assumption that compiled regex objects are instances of re.Pattern; it fails for compiled objects from the 'regex' module. The correct detection relationship is duck-typing (e.g., hasattr(value, "pattern") and using value.pattern for serialization).

5) Hardcoded use-sites to change (locations encoding wrong values/assumptions): direct calls to re.compile(...) and isinstance(..., re.Pattern) in the files above. The explicit formula found in the investigation to replace the single hardcoded call is: try re.compile(pattern) except re.error: try import regex as _regex and return _regex.compile(pattern). Also, for detection/serialization, use hasattr(value, "pattern") instead of isinstance(value, re.Pattern).

6) Error handling gap: argparse/optparse type/validator paths do not convert compilation failures into ArgumentTypeError/OptionValueError with a readable message; instead they let re.error propagate and crash the run. The assumption encoded is that re.compile either succeeds or raising re.error is acceptable at this layer.
StepsToReproduce: Create a .pylintrc in your project root containing the setting: function-rgx=[\p{Han}a-z_][\p{Han}a-z0-9_]{2,30}$
Run pylint in the project (e.g. `pylint`).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pylint accepts the regex containing Unicode property escapes (e.g. \p{Han}) as a valid configuration value and continues linting without raising an unhandled exception. If the runtime environment lacks support for the pattern, pylint should surface a clear configuration validation error rather than crashing with an internal traceback.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint fails during startup while parsing the configuration with an unhandled exception (traceback ends with `re.error: bad escape \p at position 1`). The process aborts and pylint does not proceed to lint files.

hints_text:
This doesn't seem like it is a `pylint` issue?

`re.compile("[\p{Han}a-z_]")` also raises normally. `\p` also isn't documented: https://docs.python.org/3/howto/regex.html
Is this a supported character?
I think this could be improved! Similar to the helpful output when passing an unrecognized option to Pylint, we could give a friendly output indicating that the regex pattern is invalid without the traceback; happy to put a MR together if you agree.
Thanks @mbyrnepr2 I did not even realize it was a crash that we had to fix before your comment.
@mbyrnepr2 I think in the above stacktrace on line 1858 makes the most sense.

We need to decide though if we continue to run the program. I think it makes sense to still quit. If we catch regex errors there and pass we will also "allow" ignore path regexes that don't work. I don't think we should do that.

Imo, incorrect regexes are a little different from other "incorrect" options, since there is little risk that they are valid on other interpreters or versions such as old messages etc. Therefore, I'd prefer to (cleanly) exit.
Indeed @DanielNoord I think we are on the same page regarding this point; I would also exit instead of passing if the regex is invalid. That line you mention, we can basically try/except on re.error and exit printing the details of the pattern which is invalid.

patch:
diff --git a/pylint/reporters/text.py b/pylint/reporters/text.py
--- a/pylint/reporters/text.py
+++ b/pylint/reporters/text.py
@@ -175,7 +175,7 @@ def on_set_current_module(self, module: str, filepath: str | None) -> None:
         self._template = template
 
         # Check to see if all parameters in the template are attributes of the Message
-        arguments = re.findall(r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}", template)
+        arguments = re.findall(r"\{(\w+?)(:.*)?\}", template)
         for argument in arguments:
             if argument[0] not in MESSAGE_FIELDS:
                 warnings.warn(

     """TODO remove in 3.0."""

problem_statement.Title: Text reporters mis-parse escaped-brace --msg-template (e.g. '{{ "Category": "{category}" }}')
problem_statement.Description: When a user supplies a msg-template containing literal/escaped braces (double braces to emit a literal '{' or '}'), the text reporters treat the literal content as format fields. This triggers spurious "Don't recognize the argument ..." warnings and results in the template text being stripped or corrupted in output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect field extraction regex in pylint/reporters/text.py: TextReporter.on_set_current_module. The code uses re.findall(r"\{(.+?)(:.*)?\}", template) to enumerate fields and then removes unknowns with template = re.sub(r"\{" + argument[0] + r"(:.*?)?\}", "", template). This encodes the incorrect assumption that anything between '{' and '}' is a format field and that braces are never escaped.

2) Removal regex is fragile and not escaped. The removal pattern r"\{" + argument[0] + r"(:.*?)?\}" constructs a regex from the raw argument text without re.escape, causing incorrect matches and unintended stripping of template text when argument[0] contains characters interpreted specially by regex.

3) Parsing logic is not aligned with Python str.format semantics (pylint/reporters/text.py uses MESSAGE_FIELDS to validate fields). write_message formats with self._fixed_template.format(**asdict(msg)), so discovery/validation of fields must follow Python's str.format rules (which treat '{{' and '}}' as escaped literal braces and support complex field names). The current regex approach therefore misclassifies escaped braces and complex field names.

4) Location and impact: The buggy logic is implemented in pylint/reporters/text.py: TextReporter.on_set_current_module. Because ParseableTextReporter, ColorizedTextReporter, and VSTextReporter inherit this method, they all inherit the faulty behavior.

5) Correct parsing relationship (diagnostic formula): field discovery should follow string.Formatter().parse(template), which yields tuples (literal_text, field_name, format_spec, conversion) and ignores escaped braces. For validation the root of a field_name should be obtained by splitting on r"[.\[]" (e.g. root = re.split(r"[.\[]", field_name)[0]) and checked against MESSAGE_FIELDS. Unknown fields should be removed using a regex constructed with re.escape(field_name), e.g. re.sub(r"\{" + re.escape(field_name) + r"(?:\:.*)?\}", "", template). (These relationships are identified in the investigation as the correct behavior and exact patterns to use.)

6) Tests gap: unit tests for --msg-template did not cover templates with escaped braces (e.g. '{{ ... }}'), so the regression was not caught by existing tests.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python file test.py containing any code that triggers pylint messages (e.g. an unused import).
Run: pylint test.py --msg-template='{{ "Category": "{category}" }}'
Observe the warning from pylint/reporters/text.py about an unrecognized argument and the corrupted output lines.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pylint should print each message using the provided template with literal braces preserved. For the example template '{{ "Category": "{category}" }}', the output lines should be the dictionary with "Category" as the key, e.g. { "Category": "convention" } and { "Category": "error" } for each message.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pylint emits a UserWarning: Don't recognize the argument '{ "Category"' in the --msg-template... and the printed messages are corrupted/stripped, producing lines like '" }' instead of the intended dictionary, matching the example in the bug report.

hints_text:
Subsequently, there is also this behavior with the quotes
```
$ pylint test.py --msg-template='"Category": "{category}"'
************* Module test
Category": "convention
Category": "error
Category": "error
Category": "convention
Category": "convention
Category": "error

$ pylint test.py --msg-template='""Category": "{category}""'
************* Module test
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "error"
"Category": "error"
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "convention"
"Category": "error"
```
Commit that changed the behavior was probably this one: https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/commit/7c3533ca48e69394391945de1563ef7f639cd27d#diff-76025f0bc82e83cb406321006fbca12c61a10821834a3164620fc17c978f9b7e

And I tested on 2.11.1 that it is working as intended on that version.
Thanks for digging into this !
